prompt: Read all 27 pages of Bernstein's If You Can.
title: Bernstein — If You Can (PDF)
reflectionPrompt: Which of the five hurdles is the one you're personally most behind on right now?

prompt: Bernstein's argument about WHY learning history matters in markets is best summarized as:
choices: Investors who haven't experienced a major drawdown firsthand tend to underestimate it and panic-sell when one occurs. Reading market history is a substitute for that experience.
Macro analysis is the highest-leverage activity in investing.
explanation: The argument is behavioral, not predictive. History inoculates you against the temptation to interpret a 30% drawdown as 'unprecedented' when in fact it's happened once a decade for 200 years.

prompt: Bernstein's stance on the financial advisory industry is best characterized as:
choices: The industry is well-aligned with retail investor interests.
Most fee-based advice extracts value from the client; assume adversarial alignment by default and demand evidence to the contrary.
Advisors are useful for tax planning only.
Robo-advisors solve the alignment problem entirely.
explanation: The fee structure is the alignment problem. A 1% AUM fee compounded over 30 years is structurally inconsistent with maximizing client outcomes; it maximizes advisor revenue.

prompt: Bernstein orders the five hurdles deliberately. Why is 'save enough' #1 rather than 'understand finance' or 'pick the right portfolio'?
reference: Because at the savings rates most people actually have, no realistic portfolio strategy can compensate. If you save 5% of income and need to fund 25 years of retirement, no equity allocation gets you there at sustainable risk. If you save 20%, almost any reasonable broadly-diversified portfolio gets you there. The savings rate dominates the strategy choice by an order of magnitude. Putting it first is Bernstein's way of saying: the math literally doesn't work without it, so stop trying to skip step one.

prompt: Look at Bernstein's reading list at the back of the booklet. Pick one of the five books and explain why he might have included it.
reference: The five are: Common Sense on Mutual Funds (Bogle — case for indexing + low cost), Devil Take the Hindmost (Chancellor — speculative manias through history, inoculation against 'this time is different'), Manias Panics & Crashes (Kindleberger — taxonomy of credit bubbles), Wealth War & Wisdom (Biggs — markets through political catastrophe), Your Money & Your Brain (Zweig — behavioral). Each maps to one of the five hurdles. Bogle for #5 (industry), Chancellor/Kindleberger/Biggs for #3 (history), Zweig for #4 (behavior).
